# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 83

This is turn 83. Turns 1 through 82 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 83.

## Turn 83 — the critical Frattini block is now formal

The candidate remains unchanged:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

The tame relation is implicit. Proof-level exponents mean the genuine profinite idempotent \(\omega _2\); \(40491355905\) is only its required residue modulo \(85667662080\). 

Turn 82 made the deformation duality and Fourier character calculation rigorous by finite Heisenberg reduction. Its remaining group-theoretic obligation was an exact critical-block lemma. 

### Critical-block theorem

Fix a tame frame
\[
\alpha:T\twoheadrightarrow H=G/L,
\]
with \(L\triangleleft G\) a finite \(2\)-group. Let \(H_2\) be the maximal \(2\)-quotient of \(H\), and put
\[
N=\ker(H\to H_2),
\]
so \(N\) has odd order.

Choose
\[
S\triangleleft P\triangleleft G
\]
such that every \(G\)-chief factor below \(S\) is trivial, while
\[
P/S\simeq V
\]
is the first nontrivial simple characteristic-two factor. Choose \(K\triangleleft G\), \(K\leq P\), minimally subject to
\[
KS=P.
\]

Then, writing
\[
R=\Phi(K),\qquad M=K/R,\qquad T_0=(K\cap S)/R,
\]
the following hold uniformly:

\[
R\leq Z(K),\qquad R^2=1,\qquad K^4=1,
\]
\[
T_0=\operatorname{rad}(M),\qquad \operatorname{head}(M)\simeq V,
\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{G/K}(M,\mathbf F_2)=0,
\]
and every nonzero invariant scalar pushout of \(R\) restricts to a nonsingular determinant extension of \(V\).

### 1. Odd automorphisms centralize the scalar run

Let \(\widetilde N\) be an odd Hall complement in the preimage of \(N\) in \(G\). Choose a \(G\)-chief series
\[
1=S_0\triangleleft S_1\triangleleft\cdots\triangleleft S_m=S.
\]
Because every factor \(S_i/S_{i-1}\) is trivial,
\[
[S_i,\widetilde N]\leq S_{i-1}.
\]
Hence
\[
[S,{}_{m}\widetilde N]=1.
\]

Coprime action gives
\[
[S,\widetilde N]=[S,\widetilde N,\widetilde N]
               =\cdots
               =[S,{}_{m}\widetilde N]=1.
\tag{83.1}
\]

Thus the odd Hall lift centralizes the entire scalar run, not merely its chief factors.

On the other hand, \(N\) acts nontrivially on \(V\). Otherwise \(V\) would factor through the \(2\)-group \(H_2\), whose only simple \(\mathbf F_2\)-module is trivial.

### 2. Thompson criticality forces the class-two collapse

Use Thompson’s \(2\)-critical-subgroup theorem in the precise form:

> Every finite \(2\)-group \(K\) has a characteristic subgroup \(C\) such that  
> \[
> C/Z(C)\text{ is elementary abelian},\qquad C^4=1,
> \]
> and the kernel of
> \[
> \operatorname{Aut}(K)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}(C)
> \]
> is a \(2\)-group.

The odd group \(\widetilde N\) acts nontrivially on \(K/(K\cap S)\simeq V\), hence nontrivially on \(K\). It cannot centralize \(C\), because its resulting nontrivial odd image in \(\operatorname{Aut}(K)\) cannot lie in the \(2\)-group kernel above.

By (83.1), \(\widetilde N\) centralizes \(S\). Therefore \(C\nleq S\). Since \(C\) is characteristic in the \(G\)-normal subgroup \(K\), it is \(G\)-normal, and
\[
CS/S
\]
is a nonzero \(G\)-submodule of the simple module \(P/S\). Hence
\[
CS=P.
\]
Minimality of \(K\) now forces
\[
C=K.
\]

Consequently
\[
K/Z(K)\text{ is elementary abelian},\qquad K^4=1.
\]
Thus \(K\) has class at most two and
\[
R=\Phi(K)=K^2[K,K]\leq Z(K).
\]
Moreover \(K^2\) and \([K,K]\) both have exponent two, so
\[
R^2=1.
\tag{83.2}
\]

Here \(R\) is central in \(K\), but need not be central in all of \(G\). That distinction causes no problem: Turn 82’s deformation theorem applies to arbitrary elementary \(G/R\)-modules, and only invariant characters of \(R\) are used for scalar pushouts. This sharpens the wording of Turn 80. 

### 3. The Frattini quotient has simple head

Because
\[
K/(K\cap S)\simeq V
\]
is elementary abelian, \(R\leq K\cap S\), and
\[
0\longrightarrow T_0\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow V\longrightarrow0.
\]

Since \(M/T_0\simeq V\) is simple,
\[
\operatorname{rad}(M)\leq T_0.
\]
If a maximal submodule \(X<M\) failed to contain \(T_0\), then \(X+T_0=M\). Its preimage \(K_X<K\) would be a proper \(G\)-normal subgroup satisfying
\[
K_XS=P,
\]
contradicting the minimality of \(K\). Therefore
\[
T_0=\operatorname{rad}(M),\qquad \operatorname{head}(M)\simeq V.
\tag{83.3}
\]

Since \(V\) is nontrivial,
\[
\operatorname{Hom}_{G/K}(M,\mathbf F_2)=0.
\tag{83.4}
\]

This also proves decoration invisibility. If
\[
\theta:G\to E
\]
is an elementary scalar decoration, then \(\theta\) kills
\[
\Phi(K)=K^2[K,K],
\]
so \(\theta|_K\) factors through a \(G/K\)-map \(M\to E\). Equation (83.4) gives
\[
\theta(K)=0.
\tag{83.5}
\]

### 4. Every invariant scalar pushout is a determinant pushout

Take
\[
0\neq\lambda\in (R^\vee)^{G/R}.
\]
Then
\[
K_\lambda=K/\ker\lambda
\]
is a nonsplit central extension
\[
1\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2\longrightarrow K_\lambda
\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow1.
\]
It is nonsplit because
\[
\ker\lambda\leq\Phi(K),\qquad
\Phi(K_\lambda)=R/\ker\lambda\neq1.
\]

Let
\[
q_\lambda:M\to\mathbf F_2
\]
be its square map and \(b_\lambda\) its polar form. The adjoint
\[
b_\lambda^\flat:M\to M^\vee
\]
kills \(T_0\): all composition factors of \(T_0\) are trivial, whereas
\[
\operatorname{soc}(M^\vee)
 \simeq \operatorname{head}(M)^\vee
 \simeq V^\vee
\]
is nontrivial simple. Hence
\[
b_\lambda(T_0,M)=0.
\tag{83.6}
\]

It remains to prove
\[
q_\lambda|_{T_0}=0.
\tag{83.7}
\]

Suppose instead that this restriction is nonzero. Quotient by its kernel to obtain
\[
0\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2t\longrightarrow\overline M
\longrightarrow V\longrightarrow0,
\]
where \(t\) lies in the polar radical and
\[
\overline q(t)=1.
\]

The lower wild \(2\)-kernel acts trivially on \(t\) and on \(V\). Any residual action therefore has shear form
\[
x\longmapsto x+f(\bar x)t.
\]
But invariance of \(\overline q\) gives
\[
\overline q(x+f(\bar x)t)
 =\overline q(x)+f(\bar x),
\]
so \(f=0\). Thus \(\overline M\) factors through the tame quotient \(H\).

Now
\[
H^1(H,V^\vee)=0.
\]
For ramified \(V\), tame inertia has odd order and \(V^I=0\). For nontrivial unramified \(V\), Frobenius minus the identity is invertible, and cyclic cohomology vanishes. Therefore
\[
\overline M\simeq\mathbf F_2\oplus V.
\]
Composing \(M\twoheadrightarrow\overline M\) with projection onto \(\mathbf F_2\) contradicts (83.4). This proves (83.7).

Equations (83.6)–(83.7) show that \(q_\lambda\) descends to an invariant quadratic form
\[
\bar q_\lambda:V\to\mathbf F_2.
\]
It is nonzero, since otherwise \(K_\lambda\) would be elementary abelian and the extension would split. Its polar radical is a submodule of the simple module \(V\). A zero polar form would make \(\bar q_\lambda\) an invariant linear form, which is impossible. Hence
\[
\bar q_\lambda
\]
is nonsingular.

Thus every nonzero invariant character of the Frattini kernel produces exactly the determinant obstruction already handled by the Wall–Arf and local deep-unit theorems.

### 5. Combination with finite-Heisenberg duality

Turn 82 now applies without any residual formal assumption.

For the elementary top layer \(M\),
\[
\operatorname{Ob}_\rho(M)^\vee
 \simeq (M^\vee)^{G/K}=0.
\]
Therefore every generated lower map lifts through \(M\), with exactly
\[
2^{2\dim M}
\]
relation-compatible lifts. Proper-image corrections have a strictly smaller wild kernel and retain the fixed tame frame.

For the elementary Frattini kernel \(R\), put
\[
D=(R^\vee)^{G/R}.
\]
Turn 82 identifies \(D\) with the full character group of the relation-obstruction space. If \(X_\Gamma\) is the fixed-frame lower-map set and
\[
m_{\Gamma,\lambda}
 =\#\{\rho\in X_\Gamma:
       \langle\lambda,o_\Gamma(\rho)\rangle=0\},
\]
then finite Fourier inversion gives
\[
\#\{\rho:o_\Gamma(\rho)=0\}
 =
 \frac1{|D|}
 \sum_{\lambda\in D}
 \bigl(2m_{\Gamma,\lambda}-|X_\Gamma|\bigr).
\tag{83.8}
\]

For \(\lambda=0\), equality follows from induction on the smaller wild kernel. For every \(\lambda\neq0\), the theorem above reduces the pushout to the already-closed nonsingular determinant problem on \(V\), with any filtration-one edge handled by perfect deformation duality and any mixed edge removed by shearing. Thus every term of (83.8) agrees for the candidate and the local group.

Once the obstruction vanishes, Turn 82 supplies the same \(R\)-lift multiplicity. Decorations impose no condition by (83.5). Every lift remains surjective: if its image \(J\) maps onto \(G/R\), then
\[
(J\cap K)R=K.
\]
Since \(R=\Phi(K)\), this forces \(J\cap K=K\), and hence \(J=G\).

Therefore the mixed scalar/non-scalar critical block is uniformly closed. The conclusion is structural: the scalar radical may enlarge \(K/\Phi(K)\), but it cannot create a deeper nonlinear Frattini tower or an additional obstruction invariant.

**ESTABLISHED**: Formalized the critical-block theorem requested in Turn 82. Proved that an odd Hall lift centralizes the entire scalar chief run; Thompson criticality and minimality force \(K\) to have class at most two and exponent at most four, with \(\Phi(K)\) elementary and central in \(K\). Proved the simple-head determinant lemma and combined it with finite-Heisenberg deformation duality and Fourier inversion to close the complete critical block, including decorations and surjectivity.

**GAP**: The private 5402-group verifier remains unrun. A final proof note must cite or reproduce Thompson’s critical-subgroup theorem in the exact form used above; this is now the only substantial external finite-group input, not an open finite-quotient branch.

**NEXT**: State the fixed-tame-frame induction as one theorem using this critical-block result and Turn 82’s finite-Heisenberg duality, sum over tame frames, specialize to \(L=O_2(G)\), and submit the unchanged \(A_2\) residue block.

[BREAKTHROUGH]
